Course Title: Human Override: Knowing When to Step In
Category: Agriculture & Resources: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing, Mining, Energy
Description: Why human intervention remains essential in AI-assisted operations.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with day-to-day operations in at least one agricultural, forestry, fishing, mining, or energy sector is helpful, though no prior experience with AI or automated systems is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify situations in agriculture, forestry, fishing, mining, and energy operations where AI-assisted systems require human oversight, and explain the key risks that arise when automated decision-making operates without appropriate human checks. You will also apply practical judgement to recognise the warning signs that indicate when to intervene, ensuring that critical operational decisions in resource-based industries remain safe, responsible, and effective.

lessons.lesson title: Human Override: Knowing When to Step In
lessons.Description: This lesson explores why human judgement remains a critical part of any AI-assisted workflow, even as automation becomes more capable and widespread. You will learn to recognise the situations where relying solely on AI output can introduce risk, and how to identify the moments that call for a human to step in and take control. Through practical examples, the lesson helps you build confidence in questioning, reviewing, and overriding AI-generated decisions when the context demands it. By the end, you will have a clearer sense of your own role as an active participant in AI-assisted processes, rather than a passive observer. This is a beginner-friendly lesson designed to give you the knowledge and awareness to work alongside AI tools more responsibly and effectively.
lessons.content: Why human intervention remains essential in AI-assisted operations.

slides.subtitle: Recognising the limits of AI systems
slides.slidecontent:
• AI makes mistakes
• Context often gets missed
• Humans catch critical errors
• Judgement matters most
• You are the safeguard

slides.subtitle: Understanding where automation breaks down
slides.slidecontent:
• Trained on past data, struggles with change
• Confidently wrong with no self-awareness
• Missing nuance, tone, cultural context
• Poor handling of edge cases
• No understanding of real consequences

slides.slidetitle: AI's Blind Spots in Resource Industries
slides.subtitle: Specific risks in agriculture, mining, and energy operations
slides.slidecontent:
• Weather patterns change faster than training updates
• Equipment conditions vary by location and age
• Safety protocols require human interpretation
• Environmental factors need local knowledge
• Regulatory changes happen in real-time

slides.subtitle: When AI signals need human attention
slides.slidecontent:
• Overly confident outputs from incomplete data
• Repeated errors in the same area
• Results contradicting known facts or policies
• Sudden shifts in tone or logic
• System flags uncertainty or incomplete responses
• Your gut feeling that something is off

slides.subtitle: Red flags that demand immediate human review
slides.slidecontent:
• Numbers that don't pass basic sense checks
• Recommendations ignoring safety protocols
• Solutions assuming perfect conditions
• Advice contradicting recent field experience
• Outputs that seem suspiciously perfect

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to strengthen decision-making
slides.slidecontent:
• Practice with realistic scenarios regularly
• Identify the shift from helpful to risky
• Rehearse responses out loud or in writing
• Reflect on past hesitations to learn patterns
• Trust your instincts as valid data points

slides.subtitle: Applying override judgement to real situations
slides.slidecontent:
• AI recommends equipment changes mid-season
• System suggests ignoring weather warnings
• Algorithm proposes cost cuts affecting safety
• Automated schedule conflicts with crew limits
• Output assumes resources that aren't available

slides.subtitle: The essentials worth keeping in mind
slides.slidecontent:
• AI makes mistakes
• Humans catch what AI misses
• Context matters most
• Trust your instincts
• Step in early, step in often
• You are the final check

scripts.script: Welcome to this lesson on human override, and specifically, when AI needs your guidance. Now, AI systems are genuinely impressive, but they are not infallible. They make mistakes, sometimes small ones, sometimes significant ones, and quite often they miss the broader context of a situation. They process information, but they do not truly understand it the way you do. That is where you come in. Human oversight is not just a nice extra layer, it is an essential part of working responsibly with AI. Your ability to spot something that feels off, to question an output, or to recognise when a decision carries real consequences, that is something no AI can replicate. Throughout this lesson, we are going to explore how to develop that critical eye, so you know exactly when to step in and take control. Think of yourself not as someone who simply uses AI, but as the safeguard that keeps it on track.

So let's talk about where AI actually falls short, because this is really important. AI systems learn from historical data, which means when the world changes quickly, they can be slow to catch up. And here's the tricky part: AI doesn't always know when it's wrong. It can give you an answer that sounds completely confident and authoritative, even when the information is outdated or just plain inaccurate. There's also the question of nuance. Things like tone, cultural sensitivity, and context are genuinely difficult for AI to get right. Then there are edge cases, those unusual or unexpected situations that simply don't fit neatly into the patterns the system was trained on. AI tends to struggle with those. And perhaps most importantly, AI has no real understanding of consequences. It doesn't weigh up the impact of what it's suggesting. That's why human judgement isn't just helpful in these moments, it's absolutely essential.

In resource-based industries like agriculture, mining, and energy, these AI limitations take on particular significance. Weather patterns shift faster than most training data can be updated, but AI might still recommend planting schedules based on last decade's rainfall. Equipment conditions vary dramatically by location, age, and usage, yet AI often assumes standard performance across the board. Safety protocols need human interpretation because they were written by people who understand real-world consequences, not statistical patterns. Environmental factors require local knowledge that no algorithm can capture, and regulatory changes happen in real-time while AI training cycles take months. These aren't abstract problems, they're daily realities that make human oversight absolutely critical in your sector.

So how do you actually know when it's time to step in? There are a few warning signs worth watching for. One of the most common is when an AI gives you a very confident-sounding answer, but the information it was working with was incomplete or unclear. Confidence doesn't always mean correctness. You should also pay attention if the same type of error keeps cropping up, or if the output contradicts something you know to be true, whether that's a recent event or your organisation's own policies. Sometimes the signal is more subtle, like a sudden shift in tone or logic that doesn't quite make sense. And occasionally, the system itself will flag uncertainty through low confidence scores or incomplete responses, so do take those seriously. Finally, don't dismiss your instincts. If something feels off, that gut feeling is often worth investigating. These signals are your cues to pause and take a closer look.

Beyond general warning signs, there are specific red flags that should trigger immediate human review. If numbers appear in AI output, do a quick sense check, because systems can be confidently wrong about figures that would be obviously incorrect to someone with field experience. Watch for recommendations that seem to ignore established safety protocols or assume perfect operating conditions that rarely exist in real operations. Be particularly cautious when AI advice contradicts recent experience from your team or location, as the system may not have access to that current information. And be suspicious of anything that looks absolutely perfect. Real-world solutions usually involve trade-offs and compromises, so flawless recommendations often signal that the AI has missed something important about the complexity of your situation.

Building confidence in override decisions is genuinely a skill you can develop, and like most skills, it improves with practice. Work through realistic scenarios regularly, the kind where you need to spot when an AI response crosses from useful into potentially risky territory. Practice articulating your concerns out loud or in writing, because this trains your brain to respond with confidence rather than hesitation. When you find yourself hesitating to override a system, take a moment to understand what's holding you back. Often it's not that your instincts are wrong, but that you haven't learned to trust them yet. Remember that your gut feeling is actually your brain processing subtle cues that don't fit into neat categories. That intuition, combined with your experience and knowledge of local conditions, is exactly what makes human oversight so valuable.

Let's work through some realistic scenarios you might encounter. Imagine AI recommends changing equipment settings mid-season based on efficiency data, but you know your particular machinery behaves differently in current field conditions. Or the system suggests proceeding despite weather warnings because historical data shows low risk, but your local weather patterns have shifted recently. Consider an algorithm proposing cost cuts that would affect safety measures, confident in its calculations but unaware of regulatory requirements. What about automated scheduling that conflicts with crew availability or legal work hour limits? These scenarios help you practice identifying the moment when AI confidence meets real-world complexity, and where your expertise becomes essential for safe, effective decisions.

So before we move on, let's just take a moment to pull together the essentials from everything we've covered. The core message is this: AI is a powerful tool, but it is not infallible. It makes mistakes, and quite often those mistakes are the kind that only a human would notice, because we understand context in a way that AI simply does not. When something feels off, trust that instinct. You do not need to wait until a problem becomes obvious before you step in. The earlier you intervene, the easier it is to correct course. And throughout all of this, remember that you are the final check. Not the system, not the algorithm, not the automation. You. That responsibility might feel like a lot, but it is also genuinely empowering. You bring judgment, experience, and common sense to the table, and those are things no AI can replicate. Keep that in mind every time you work alongside these tools.

Who is this for: Farm managers, agricultural workers, forestry supervisors, field technicians, mining operators, plant supervisors, energy facility operators, equipment operators, production supervisors, site managers
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Production, Field Operations, Plant Operations, Safety, Quality Control
